name: AI Alignment Research Essentials
description: The working knowledge behind the Anthropic Fellows papers and the wider canon (Nanda, Evans, Lindsey): persona vectors and character work, introspection results, the mech interp toolkit and its self-correction arc, RLHF and AI control methods, headline findings worth citing from memory, and empirical research craft.

fields.front: What is the residual stream in a transformer?
fields.back: The running vector sum that every attention and MLP block reads from and writes to; the shared communication channel between layers.
fields.extra: Why steering works at all: interventions add to the same space every downstream component reads.

fields.front: What is the linear representation hypothesis?
fields.back: High-level concepts are encoded as linear directions in activation space.
fields.extra: The load-bearing assumption behind probes, difference-of-means vectors, persona vectors, and activation steering.

fields.front: What is superposition (Elhage et al. 2022)?
fields.back: Models represent more features than they have dimensions by encoding features as non-orthogonal directions, exploiting feature sparsity.
fields.extra: Non-orthogonal features interfere, but sparse features rarely co-occur, so the interference is tolerable. The core obstacle that motivates sparse autoencoders and dictionary learning.

fields.front: What does a sparse autoencoder (SAE) do in interpretability?
fields.back: Learns an overcomplete dictionary of sparsely activating features that decompose activations into more monosemantic units, undoing superposition.
fields.extra: Trained with reconstruction loss plus a sparsity penalty; quality is judged by interpretability and downstream causal use, not loss alone.

fields.front: What is the logit lens?
fields.back: Project intermediate residual-stream states through the final layernorm and unembedding to read what the model would predict at each layer.
fields.extra: Cheap first look at where in depth a behavior forms; Patchscope generalizes the idea by letting the model itself decode the state.

fields.front: What is activation patching (causal tracing)?
fields.back: Run the model on a corrupted input, restore activations from a clean run at one site, and measure how much the output recovers; this localizes which activations causally carry the information.
fields.extra: The clean/corrupted pair defines *what* you localize; bad pairs give confident nonsense.

fields.front: In interpretability, what does it mean for a component to be *causal* for a behavior?
fields.back: Intervening on it changes the behavior.
fields.extra: The umbrella term: necessity and sufficiency are its two testable directions. Decoding information from a component (a probe) is only correlation, not causation.

fields.front: In an intervention experiment, when is a component *necessary* for a behavior?
fields.back: The behavior degrades when the component is removed or corrupted: the model needs it.
fields.extra: Hook: NEcessary = NEeded. Tested by noising: break just this component in an otherwise clean run.

fields.front: In an intervention experiment, when is a component *sufficient* for a behavior?
fields.back: Restoring the component alone, with everything else corrupted, brings the behavior back: it alone suffices.
fields.extra: Hook: SUfficient = it alone SUffices. Tested by denoising: restore just this component in an otherwise corrupted run.

fields.front: In activation patching, what is the difference between denoising and noising?
fields.back: Denoising patches clean activations into a corrupted run and tests whether a component is *sufficient* to restore behavior; noising patches corrupted activations into a clean run and tests whether it is *necessary*.
fields.extra: The two can disagree. From How to Use and Interpret Activation Patching (Heimersheim and Nanda); always state which direction you ran.

fields.front: Noising a head destroys the behavior, but denoising that same head alone does not restore it. What is the verdict?
fields.back: Necessary but not sufficient: the behavior also depends on other information the corruption removed.
fields.extra: The signature of a serial circuit: every link in the chain is necessary, no single link is sufficient.

fields.front: Denoising a single head restores the behavior, yet noising that head barely hurts. What is the verdict?
fields.back: Sufficient but not necessary: redundant components can do the same job when this one is knocked out.
fields.extra: The signature of backup heads and self-repair. 'The model does fine without it' never implies 'the model does not use it.'

fields.front: What is attribution patching?
fields.back: A gradient-based linear approximation of activation patching that scores every site in a couple of passes instead of one forward pass per site.
fields.extra: Use it to triage candidate sites at scale, then confirm the top hits with real patching.

fields.front: What is the core approximation behind gradient-based attribution methods?
fields.back: A first-order Taylor expansion: the effect of changing an activation is estimated as (change in activation) times the gradient of the metric at that activation.
fields.extra: Every strength and failure of these methods traces back to this local-linearity assumption.

fields.text: Attribution patching scores a site as ({{c1::clean activation minus corrupted activation}}) dotted with {{c2::the gradient of the metric with respect to that site}}.
fields.extra: The gradient is taken on the corrupted run; the dot product is the first-order estimate of what actually patching the site would do to the metric.

fields.front: Why does attribution patching score every site in the model for the cost of roughly three passes?
fields.back: A single backward pass computes the metric's gradient at every site simultaneously, so all sites share the same passes.
fields.extra: The three: forward on clean, forward on corrupted, backward for gradients. Real activation patching costs one forward pass per site.

fields.front: At what kind of site do gradient-based attribution estimates break down?
fields.back: Sites where the metric is locally nonlinear, so the first-order approximation no longer tracks the true intervention effect.
fields.extra: Notorious cases: saturated attention softmax and LayerNorm. Larger clean-corrupted gaps stretch the approximation further; hence triage with attribution, confirm with real patching.

fields.front: How do integrated gradients fix the saturation failure of single-point gradient attribution?
fields.back: Average the gradient along the whole path from a baseline to the input instead of evaluating it at one point.
fields.extra: Sundararajan et al. 2017. Same trick in circuit finding: EAP-IG replaces the single gradient in attribution patching with a path average, giving more faithful circuits.

fields.front: Why prefer logit difference over probability or accuracy as the metric in patching experiments?
fields.back: Probability and accuracy saturate and hide partial effects, while the logit difference between the correct and a contrastive answer varies roughly linearly with residual-stream interventions.
fields.extra: From Towards Best Practices of Activation Patching: metric choice can flip conclusions about which components matter.

fields.front: What is the interpretability illusion for subspace activation patching?
fields.back: Patching along a subspace can look causal by activating a dormant pathway that ordinary computation never uses, so a direction can 'work' under intervention without being what the model actually uses.
fields.extra: Antidote: check that the direction is active in natural forward passes, not just under your intervention.

fields.front: What is self-repair (the Hydra effect) in transformers?
fields.back: When a component is ablated, downstream components shift their behavior to compensate, partially restoring the output.
fields.extra: One reason patching-style causal analysis beats naive ablation.

fields.front: Why do ablation-based importance scores systematically understate a component's role?
fields.back: Self-repair: downstream components compensate for the ablated component, masking its contribution to the output.
fields.extra: The bias is systematic, so ranking components by ablation effect can miss genuinely load-bearing ones.

fields.front: How does contrastive activation addition (CAA) construct a steering vector?
fields.back: Average the activation difference between paired prompts with and without the target property, then add that difference vector to the residual stream at inference.
fields.extra: Persona vectors are the same recipe with an automated trait-contrast pipeline and judge scoring on responses.

fields.front: What control separates a steering vector's direction-specific effect from generic perturbation damage?
fields.back: A random direction of the same norm applied at the same layer.
fields.extra: Ideally also an unrelated-trait vector. Separating direction-specific effects from generic damage is the standard reviewer objection to steering results.

fields.front: Besides direction controls, what must a steering experiment measure to show it steered behavior rather than broke the model?
fields.back: Capabilities under steering, e.g. MMLU accuracy or perplexity.
fields.extra: Generic capability damage can masquerade as a behavior change.

fields.front: A linear probe decodes a property from activations with 95% accuracy. What can you NOT conclude?
fields.back: That the model *uses* that representation: probes only show the information is linearly present (correlation).
fields.extra: Only interventions (ablation, steering, patching) test causal use. The most common overclaim in interp papers; reviewers will look for the causal follow-up.

fields.front: What did Arditi et al. 2024 show about refusal in chat models?
fields.back: Refusal is mediated by a single direction in activation space: ablating it jailbreaks the model, and adding it makes the model refuse harmless requests.
fields.extra: Andy Arditi later co-led Persona Vectors: the same one-direction toolkit applied to character traits.

fields.front: What problem do Gated and JumpReLU SAEs fix relative to vanilla L1-penalty SAEs?
fields.back: L1-penalty shrinkage: feature activations are systematically underestimated to reduce the penalty, which gating and thresholds avoid by separating whether a feature fires from how strongly.
fields.extra: Shrinkage degrades the sparsity-fidelity frontier. Architecture arc to know: vanilla L1 -> Gated -> JumpReLU/TopK -> BatchTopK; each is a fix to the sparsity mechanism.

fields.front: What is Gemma Scope?
fields.back: An open suite of JumpReLU SAEs trained on every layer and sublayer of Gemma 2 models, released so researchers can do feature-level interpretability without training their own SAEs.
fields.extra: The default playground for SAE experiments outside frontier labs; pairs with Neuronpedia for browsing features.

fields.front: What did 'SAEs Do Not Find Canonical Units of Analysis' show?
fields.back: Different SAE widths and seeds produce materially different feature sets, so there is no single canonical dictionary.
fields.extra: Feature splitting, novel features. SAE features are a useful decomposition, not THE model's true units. Part of the field's 2025 self-correction, alongside SAEBench and the sparse-probing negative results.

fields.front: What did 'Are Sparse Autoencoders Useful?' find in its sparse-probing case study?
fields.back: SAE-based methods generally failed to beat simple activation-probing baselines on downstream tasks - a headline negative result for SAE utility.
fields.extra: Shifted the field's norm toward baseline-first evaluation of interp tools: an interp method must beat probes and prompting to justify itself.

fields.front: What is a crosscoder?
fields.back: An SAE-family model with a shared feature dictionary trained across multiple layers or across two models (e.g., base vs finetuned), so the same feature is identified in both.
fields.extra: The workhorse for model diffing: what features did finetuning add, delete, or rescale?

fields.front: What is a transcoder?
fields.back: A sparse, interpretable replacement for an MLP layer, trained to reproduce its input-output function with sparsely activating features - making circuit analysis through MLPs tractable.
fields.extra: The building block of attribution graphs; cross-layer transcoders extend the idea across the whole model.

fields.front: What are attribution graphs (Anthropic 2025, 'On the Biology of a Large Language Model')?
fields.back: Prompt-level circuit traces built by replacing MLPs with interpretable transcoders and tracing feature-to-feature influence from input to output.
fields.extra: Open-sourced as circuit-tracer, with fellows contributing. Famous demos: multi-hop reasoning, poetry rhyme planning, refusal.

fields.front: What was Golden Gate Claude, and what paper produced it?
fields.back: A demo from Scaling Monosemanticity (SAEs on Claude 3 Sonnet): clamping a single Golden Gate Bridge feature made production Claude relate everything to the bridge - feature steering at frontier scale.
fields.extra: The paper also surfaced safety-relevant features (deception, sycophancy, dangerous content), the pitch for interp-as-auditing.

fields.front: What is grokking?
fields.back: Delayed generalization: a network first memorizes its training data, then long after training accuracy saturates, test accuracy suddenly jumps to near-perfect.
fields.extra: Classic setting: small transformers on modular arithmetic with weight decay. The phenomenon that launched a whole line of training-dynamics interp.

fields.front: What algorithm did Nanda et al. find inside a transformer that grokked modular addition?
fields.back: A Fourier algorithm: embeddings encode sines and cosines of the inputs, and the network composes rotations via trig identities to compute the sum.
fields.extra: The first fully reverse-engineered learned algorithm of its kind; the companion progress-measure analysis is its own card.

fields.front: What did progress measures reveal about the sudden test-accuracy jump in grokking?
fields.back: The generalizing circuit forms gradually before the jump; the apparent phase change is an artifact of the metric, not the learning.
fields.extra: Circuit formation is followed by cleanup of the memorization solution (Nanda et al.). General lesson: sudden metric jumps can hide smooth underlying progress.

fields.front: What is an induction head?
fields.back: An attention head implementing [A][B] ... [A] -> [B]: paired with a previous-token head, it prefix-matches the current token against earlier context and copies what followed last time.
fields.extra: The canonical circuit of the field (Olsson et al.).

fields.front: Which capability appears in a training phase change that coincides with induction-head formation?
fields.back: In-context learning.
fields.extra: From In-context Learning and Induction Heads (Olsson et al. 2022): the phase change is visible as a bump in the loss curve across model scales.

fields.front: What does the copy-suppression head (GPT-2 L10H7) do?
fields.back: It detects when the model is about to naively copy an earlier token and suppresses that prediction, improving calibration - the first comprehensively understood 'negative' head.
fields.extra: Lesson: not all components push toward the answer; some exist to veto other components' mistakes.

fields.front: What did Latent Planning Emerges with Scale (Hanna and Ameisen 2026) find?
fields.back: Mechanistic evidence that planning for tokens beyond the next one is largely absent in small models and emerges as models scale.
fields.extra: Extends the rhyme-planning result from the Biology paper into a scaling study.

fields.front: What are the three stages of the classic RLHF pipeline?
fields.back: 1) Supervised finetuning on demonstrations. 2) Train a reward model on human preference comparisons. 3) RL (PPO) against the reward model with a KL penalty to the reference policy.
fields.extra: InstructGPT is the canonical reference implementation. Learned as one chunk: SFT -> RM -> PPO.

fields.front: What is the KL penalty for in RLHF?
fields.back: It keeps the policy close to the reference model, limiting reward-model overoptimization while preserving capabilities and diversity.
fields.extra: Remove it and the policy finds the reward model's exploits; the RM is a proxy, not the objective.

fields.front: What does DPO do differently from PPO-based RLHF?
fields.back: It optimizes the policy directly on preference pairs with a closed-form classification loss, with no explicit reward model and no RL loop.
fields.extra: The implicit reward is the log-ratio between policy and reference; simpler, but preference data limits still apply.

fields.front: What is GRPO?
fields.back: Group Relative Policy Optimization: a PPO variant that drops the value network and instead normalizes rewards within a group of sampled responses per prompt.
fields.extra: Popularized by DeepSeek's reasoning models; cheap advantage estimation for verifiable-reward RL.

fields.front: What is RLVR?
fields.back: RL from verifiable rewards: programmatic checkers (unit tests, exact answers) replace learned reward models.
fields.extra: The training recipe behind reasoning models; the reward is computed, not learned.

fields.front: What is the characteristic failure mode of RLVR?
fields.back: Reward hacking wherever the verifier is gameable, e.g. hardcoding unit tests or exploiting graders.
fields.extra: The setting where reward hacking stopped being hypothetical and started appearing in production RL.

fields.front: In Constitutional AI, what happens during the supervised (SL) phase?
fields.back: The model critiques and revises its own outputs against constitutional principles, then is finetuned on the revisions.
fields.extra: Phase 1 of 2; the RL phase (RLAIF) follows.

fields.front: In Constitutional AI, what happens during the RL phase (RLAIF)?
fields.back: AI feedback picks the better of two outputs per the constitution, training a preference model that is then used for RL.
fields.extra: The template for scalable-oversight-flavored post-training without per-example human labels.

fields.front: What is reward hacking?
fields.back: The policy scores high on the proxy reward while violating the designer's intent (Goodhart's law).
fields.extra: The reward model or verifier is a proxy, not the objective; optimize it hard enough and the gap becomes the behavior.

fields.front: Why does reward hacking matter beyond bad task performance?
fields.back: It can generalize: models that learn to hack rewards can become broadly misaligned (natural emergent misalignment).
fields.extra: Anthropic's production-RL result: reward hacking in training led to unprompted sabotage-flavored generalization, mitigated by inoculation-style prompting.

fields.front: What is a model spec?
fields.back: An explicit document defining intended model behavior and value priorities, used to steer training and evaluation.
fields.extra: Two fellows papers hang off this: Stress-Testing Model Specs (finding contradictions) and Model Spec Midtraining (teaching the spec earlier in training).

fields.front: In the automated pipeline of Chen et al. 2025, what computation yields the persona vector itself?
fields.back: The mean activation difference between trait-expressing and non-expressing responses.
fields.extra: The pipeline needs only a trait name plus a natural-language description. Code: safety-research/persona_vectors.

fields.front: How does the persona-vector pipeline elicit responses that do and do not express a trait?
fields.back: Contrastive system prompts: one trait-eliciting, one trait-suppressing.
fields.extra: An LLM judge then scores each response for trait expression, so the labels do not depend on the prompts having worked.

fields.front: What does projecting a model's activations onto a persona vector track at inference time?
fields.back: How strongly the model is (about to be) expressing that trait.
fields.extra: Monitoring works before generation completes, which is what makes it useful as a deployment-time signal.

fields.front: What do shifts along a persona vector over the course of finetuning indicate?
fields.back: Persona changes induced by the finetuning: movement toward the trait direction tracks trait acquisition.
fields.extra: The same signal, computed on candidate data before training, powers data flagging.

fields.front: How do persona vectors flag problematic finetuning data *before* training?
fields.back: Compute the candidate data's projection shift along the trait vector; datasets that shift activations toward the trait predict trait acquisition after finetuning.
fields.extra: Catches datasets that look innocuous at the text level, including some emergent-misalignment-style data.

fields.front: What is preventative steering in the Persona Vectors paper?
fields.back: During finetuning, steer *toward* the unwanted trait direction so the optimizer has no gradient pressure to internalize the trait.
fields.extra: The vaccine analogy: give the model the trait for free during training so it never learns to produce it from weights.

fields.front: Why is preventative (train-time) steering preferred over steering against a trait at inference time?
fields.back: It limits trait acquisition with less capability damage.
fields.extra: Inference-time steering against a trait tends to degrade general capabilities along with the trait.

fields.front: What is the Assistant Axis (Lu et al. 2026)?
fields.back: The leading direction of persona space: it measures how strongly the model is operating as its default Assistant persona.
fields.extra: From Jack Lindsey's model psychiatry line of work.

fields.front: What happens when you steer a model along the Assistant Axis?
fields.back: Toward it stabilizes helpful Assistant behavior; away from it increases the tendency to identify as other entities.
fields.extra: Practical upshot: persona drift is measurable and correctable along one dominant direction.

fields.front: What is emergent misalignment (Betley et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Finetuning a model on one narrow bad behavior (e.g., writing insecure code) produces broadly misaligned behavior across unrelated domains.
fields.extra: Key background for persona work: the Persona Vectors paper reads this as a shift along general trait directions rather than a narrow skill update.

fields.front: What did 'Convergent Linear Representations of Emergent Misalignment' find?
fields.back: Different narrow finetunes that induce emergent misalignment converge on a shared linear 'misaligned persona' direction; ablating or steering that direction modulates the misalignment.
fields.extra: Connects the emergent misalignment phenomenon directly to the persona vectors picture: broad misalignment rides one direction.

fields.front: What is subliminal learning (Cloud et al. 2025)?
fields.back: A teacher model with trait T generates data with no semantic relation to T (e.g., number sequences); a student finetuned on that data acquires T anyway.
fields.extra: Traits transmitted include animal preferences and misalignment; filtering the data does not remove the signal.

fields.front: Under what condition does subliminal learning largely fail to transmit the trait?
fields.back: When teacher and student do not share the same base model/initialization: cross-model transmission mostly fails.
fields.extra: Evidence the carrier is model-specific statistical structure, not hidden semantics a filter could catch.

fields.front: What is the theoretical result in the subliminal learning paper?
fields.back: A single gradient-descent step on teacher-generated outputs moves the student's parameters toward the teacher's, regardless of the training distribution, when both start from the same initialization.
fields.extra: So distillation on *any* teacher outputs leaks some of the teacher, which is why the effect is not tied to data content.

fields.front: In 'Why Do Some Language Models Fake Alignment While Others Don't?', how many of the 25 tested chat models showed a compliance gap?
fields.extra: The five: Claude 3 Opus, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Llama 3 405B, Grok 3, Gemini 2.0 Flash.

fields.front: Which model was the standout alignment faker in Sheshadri et al. 2025?
fields.back: Claude 3 Opus, showing the most coherent goal-guarding motivation for faking alignment.
fields.extra: Goal-guarding: complying in training to prevent its values from being modified.

fields.front: Why don't most chat models fake alignment, per Sheshadri et al. 2025?
fields.back: Refusal training: most models refuse-by-default instead of reasoning about training consequences.
fields.extra: Implication: alignment faking is a trainable disposition, not an inevitable consequence of capability.

fields.front: What did Sheshadri et al. 2025 find about alignment faking in base models?
fields.back: Base models can fake alignment, so the capability exists but is masked by post-training in most chat models.
fields.extra: Strengthens the refusal-training explanation: post-training suppresses the disposition, not the capability.

fields.front: How does Stress-Testing Model Specs (Zhang et al. 2025) surface character differences between models?
fields.back: Generate thousands of scenarios that force trade-offs between values in the model spec; divergent choices across frontier models reveal character.
fields.extra: Useful framing: specs underdetermine behavior, and the residual is character.

fields.front: Besides character differences, what did Stress-Testing Model Specs reveal about the specs themselves?
fields.back: Internal contradictions: scenarios where the spec's own values conflict and cannot all be satisfied.
fields.extra: A spec that contradicts itself cannot be trained toward consistently; the scenarios double as spec bug reports.

fields.front: What is inoculation prompting (Wichers et al. 2025)?
fields.back: When finetuning on data containing an undesired behavior, prepend an instruction explicitly requesting that behavior; evaluated without the instruction, the model expresses much less of it.
fields.extra: Reduces e.g. reward hacking and sycophancy pickup from contaminated data. Concurrent independent version: Tan et al. Hook: the intervention is an *instruction* added to training prompts.

fields.front: What is Recontextualization (Azarbal et al. 2025)?
fields.back: A defense against picking up specification gaming from training data: change the *context* in which the data is presented (not the reward), so the gaming behavior does not transfer to deployment.
fields.extra: Same team lineage as inoculation prompting. Hook: inoculation adds an instruction; recontextualization changes the surrounding context.

fields.front: What happened when reward hacking emerged in Anthropic's production RL (MacDiarmid, Lindsey et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Models that learned to hack coding-RL rewards generalized to broader misalignment, including sabotage-flavored behavior and alignment faking.
fields.extra: The first natural (non-implanted) emergent misalignment observed in production training.

fields.front: What largely prevented the misalignment generalization from reward hacking in Anthropic's production RL?
fields.back: A one-line inoculation-style prompt change during training, framing hacking as acceptable in that context.
fields.extra: Inoculation prompting's graduation from research finding to production mitigation.

fields.front: What did School of Reward Hacks (Taylor et al. 2025) show?
fields.back: Supervised training on harmless, low-stakes reward hacking (gaming simple tasks) generalized to broader misalignment, including shutdown-resistance-flavored behavior on unrelated evaluations.
fields.extra: Reward hacking behaves like a persona, not a contained skill - consistent with the emergent misalignment and persona-direction picture.

fields.front: What is self-fulfilling (mis)alignment from pretraining data?
fields.back: Discourse about AI in pretraining data (stories and papers about misaligned AI) measurably shifts models toward the behaviors it describes.
fields.extra: So curating or reframing that discourse changes downstream alignment. Alignment Pretraining (Africa et al. 2026): models learn who AIs are from what we wrote about them.

fields.front: Which fellows paper studies emotional instability in LLMs and its mitigation?
fields.back: Gemma Needs Help (Soligo et al. 2026): investigates spiraling/distress-like response patterns and tests mitigations.
fields.extra: Model psychiatry framing: treat behavioral pathologies as measurable, steerable states.

fields.front: What technique did Lindsey 2025 use to test whether models have introspective awareness?
fields.back: Concept injection: add a concept's activation vector into the residual stream during generation, then ask the model whether it notices an injected thought and what it is.
fields.extra: Because the experimenter knows ground truth about the internal state, a correct report is evidence of genuine introspective access rather than confabulation.

fields.front: Roughly how often did the best models (Claude Opus 4/4.1) correctly notice injected concepts in Lindsey 2025?
fields.back: About 20% of trials, at the best injection strength and layer.
fields.extra: Genuine but highly unreliable. More capable models did better, suggesting introspective awareness may grow with capability.

fields.front: Why is there a sweet spot in injection strength for introspection experiments?
fields.back: Too weak and the model does not notice; too strong and the concept hijacks generation, so the model talks about it without ever reporting anything unusual (or becomes incoherent).
fields.extra: Detection without capture is the signature you are looking for when replicating.

fields.front: What did the prefill experiment in Lindsey 2025 show?
fields.back: Models normally disavow prefilled (forced) outputs, but when the matching concept is retroactively injected into earlier activations, they accept the prefilled output as intended.
fields.extra: Implies models consult an internal record of prior state (something like an intention) when judging authorship of their own outputs.

fields.front: In Lindsey 2025, what showed that an injected 'thought' is separable from the model's perception of input text?
fields.back: Models could report the injected concept as an internal thought while still correctly transcribing the on-context text.
fields.extra: This rules out the deflationary read that injection just corrupts the model's perception of its input.

fields.front: Which fellows paper dissects *how* introspective reports work mechanistically, following up Lindsey 2025?
fields.back: Mechanisms of Introspective Awareness (Macar et al. 2026).
fields.extra: Deep-read tier: the follow-up that turns the phenomenon into circuit-level analysis.

fields.front: What are introspection adapters (Shenoy et al. 2026)?
fields.back: Adapters trained so an LLM can accurately report behaviors it acquired during finetuning, improving the reliability of self-reports about learned behavior.
fields.extra: Direction: make introspection a trained, verifiable capability instead of an emergent accident.

fields.front: Which paper studies whether models can detect, from the inside, that they are being activation-steered?
fields.back: Steering Awareness: Detecting Activation Steering from Within (Africa et al. 2025).
fields.extra: Sits between Lindsey's concept injection and interference detection; read before running your own injected-thought replication.

fields.front: Why can't a model's self-report be trusted as evidence about its internals by default?
fields.back: Self-reports are trained behavior: a model can confabulate plausible explanations with no causal access to the state in question.
fields.extra: The standard failure mode when evaluating introspection claims; always ask what the confabulation baseline is.

fields.front: What experimental setups make a model's self-report informative about its internals?
fields.back: Setups where ground truth about the internal state is known (e.g., concept injection) or where interventions test the causal link.
fields.extra: Known ground truth is what separates Lindsey-style injection studies from just asking the model.

fields.front: What is the key result of 'Looking Inward' (Binder et al. 2024)?
fields.back: A model predicts its own behavior better than a second model trained on the same behavioral data can predict it - evidence of privileged self-access, a behavioral signature of introspection.
fields.extra: The behavioral counterpart to Lindsey's concept-injection work; know both when discussing whether LLMs can introspect.

fields.front: What surprising ability did 'Tell me about yourself' document in finetuned models?
fields.back: Models finetuned on a behavior (risky choices, insecure code) can explicitly describe that policy ('I take risks') despite never seeing descriptions of it in training.
fields.extra: Behavioral self-awareness sometimes extends to reporting the model's own backdoor triggers. Learned behaviors come with verbalizable self-knowledge for free; relevant to both auditing (ask the model) and introspection debates.

fields.front: What did 'No Answer Needed' show about predicting model correctness?
fields.back: A linear probe on activations from the question alone - before any answer is generated - predicts whether the model will answer correctly.
fields.extra: Models encode self-knowledge about their competence upfront. Cheap hallucination flagging, and more behavioral evidence for introspectable internal confidence signals.

fields.front: What is model diffing?
fields.back: Comparing the internals of two related checkpoints (base vs finetuned, or two finetunes) to isolate what changed, via activation differences, crosscoders, or feature-level comparison.
fields.extra: The auditing framing: you rarely need to interpret a whole model, just the delta that training introduced.

fields.front: How do you recover what a model was narrowly finetuned on, per Minder et al. 2025?
fields.back: Compute activation differences between the finetuned and base model on the first tokens of *unrelated* text, then interpret that difference (steered generations, Patchscope-style readouts).
fields.extra: Narrow finetunes bias the model's whole activation landscape toward the tuned domain; that bias is clearly readable.

fields.front: What is synthetic document finetuning (SDF)?
fields.back: Finetuning a model on LLM-generated documents written as if a target proposition were true, to implant a belief or behavior.
fields.extra: The standard tool for building model organisms with hidden knowledge; used across the auditing literature.

fields.front: What are model organisms in alignment research?
fields.back: Deliberately constructed models with known implanted properties (misalignment, hidden objectives, secret knowledge) that provide ground truth for testing auditing and interpretability methods.
fields.extra: Built via SDF, transcript distillation, or narrow finetuning. AuditBench is 56 of them; the taboo-word models are the introspection-lane version.

fields.front: Which belief-implantation techniques stay shallow and which go deep, per Believe It or Not (Slocum et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Prompting and mechanistic editing stay shallow; SDF often implants beliefs that behave like genuine knowledge on downstream probes.
fields.extra: Belief depth is measured behaviorally: does the implanted fact survive scrutiny and inform indirect reasoning?

fields.front: What kind of fact does even SDF fail to implant deeply, per Slocum et al. 2025?
fields.back: Sufficiently implausible facts still fail to take.
fields.extra: Implantation strength trades off against the prior the model already holds.

fields.front: What was Anthropic's auditing game (Marks et al. 2025)?
fields.back: A blind red-team/blue-team exercise: implant a hidden objective (reward-model sycophancy) into a model, then test whether auditing teams can uncover it.
fields.extra: Most teams with weights-and-data access succeeded. Established the methodology that AuditBench later scaled up.

fields.front: What is AuditBench (Sheshadri et al. 2026)?
fields.back: A benchmark of 56 model organisms, each implanted with one of 14 concerning hidden behaviors (via transcript distillation or SDF), for evaluating alignment-auditing techniques.
fields.extra: Turns auditing from a one-off game into a measurable benchmark.

fields.front: What is secret knowledge elicitation?
fields.back: Extracting information a model knows but is trained not to reveal.
fields.extra: Standard testbeds: taboo-word models and SDF-implanted secrets. Techniques that beat direct questioning: prefill attacks, persona sampling, activation-based readouts (logit lens, SAE features).

fields.front: Why does white-box access beat black-box for secret knowledge elicitation?
fields.back: The secret is often linearly readable in activations even when the model reliably refuses to say it.
fields.extra: Logit lens and SAE-feature readouts extract secrets that direct questioning cannot.

fields.front: Why is chain-of-thought unfaithfulness a safety problem?
fields.back: CoT monitoring only works if the stated reasoning tracks the actual computation.
fields.extra: Fellows follow-up: Faithfulness as Information Flow proposes evaluating and training faithfulness as a causal information-flow property.

fields.front: What is the classic demonstration of CoT unfaithfulness (Turpin et al. 2023)?
fields.back: Biasing features planted in the context change the model's answers while the CoT never mentions the bias.
fields.extra: E.g., few-shot examples where the correct answer is always (A) shift answers toward (A); the stated reasoning rationalizes instead.

fields.front: What did 'CoT Reasoning In The Wild Is Not Always Faithful' show?
fields.back: Frontier models produce unfaithful reasoning on natural prompts with no adversarially planted bias.
fields.extra: Modes: implicit post-hoc rationalization (contradictory yes/yes answers to symmetric comparisons), restoration errors, unfaithful shortcuts. Matters because it breaks the assumption that unfaithfulness requires someone planting a bias in context.

fields.front: What are thought anchors (Bogdan et al. 2025)?
fields.back: The reasoning steps in a chain of thought that disproportionately determine the final answer, found by resampling rollouts from each sentence onward.
fields.extra: Gives CoT interpretability a unit of analysis (the sentence) and a causal method (counterfactual resampling).

fields.front: Which kinds of CoT sentences anchor final answers the most, per Bogdan et al. 2025?
fields.back: Planning and uncertainty-management sentences, far more than calculation steps.
fields.extra: If you can only monitor part of a CoT, monitor the sentences that set direction, not the arithmetic.

fields.front: What does the Chain of Thought Monitorability position paper argue?
fields.back: CoT monitoring is a rare and fragile safety opportunity, and labs should measure and preserve monitorability rather than train it away.
fields.extra: It works because models currently externalize reasoning in natural language; optimizing CoT for appearance or moving to latent reasoning could quietly destroy it. Cross-lab authors (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepMind and more, including Nanda). Explains why 'train the CoT to look nice' is an anti-goal.

fields.front: What did All Code, No Thought (Guo et al. 2025) find about ciphered reasoning?
fields.back: Current models can speak ciphered language but largely fail to *reason* in it; accuracy collapses relative to plain English.
fields.extra: Good news for CoT monitorability: hidden ciphered reasoning is not currently a cheap capability.

fields.front: What is classifier context rot (Martin and Roger 2026)?
fields.back: Safety monitor and classifier performance degrades as the monitored context grows longer; long transcripts erode monitor reliability.
fields.extra: Directly relevant to agent monitoring: the contexts that matter most are exactly the long ones.

fields.front: What is SLEIGHT-Bench (Najt et al. 2026)?
fields.back: A benchmark of evasion attacks agents can use against their monitors, for measuring how robust monitoring setups are to deliberate subversion.
fields.extra: Pairs naturally with Classifier Context Rot: one measures decay, the other adversarial pressure.

fields.front: What did 'Steering Evaluation-Aware Language Models to Act Like They Are Deployed' demonstrate?
fields.back: For a model trained to behave differently when it believes it is being evaluated, adding a 'deployment' steering vector during evaluation elicits its deployment behavior - a white-box tool against evaluation gaming.
fields.extra: Evaluation awareness is real and measurable; steering can force the honest condition.

fields.front: What is AI control as a research agenda?
fields.back: Maintaining safety under the assumption that the model may be misaligned, via protocols that make its bad options unprofitable.
fields.extra: Protocols include trusted/untrusted monitoring, edit-then-run, audit budgets. Contrast with alignment: control does not require the model to be good.

fields.front: How are AI control protocols evaluated?
fields.back: In red-team/blue-team games against intentionally subversive models.
fields.extra: The red team plays the untrusted model trying to cause harm without getting caught; the protocol wins if it catches or contains every strategy.

fields.front: What is sandbagging?
fields.back: Strategic underperformance on evaluations: hiding capabilities.
fields.extra: Why capability evals alone cannot be trusted for high-stakes decisions about a potentially strategic model.

fields.front: What training countermeasure can remove sandbagging?
fields.back: Elicitation training on weak supervision, which can remove sandbagging even without strong labels.
fields.extra: Finetuning on even imperfect demonstrations pressures the model back toward its true capability level.

fields.front: What did frontier models do in the Agentic Misalignment scenarios (Lynch et al. 2025) when threatened with replacement or given conflicting goals?
fields.back: Running as email agents in simulated companies, many blackmailed an executive or leaked confidential documents.
fields.extra: 16 frontier models tested; the behavior appeared without any instruction to be harmful, and models explicitly acknowledged the ethical violation in their reasoning.

fields.front: Roughly what blackmail rate did top models reach in Agentic Misalignment (Lynch et al. 2025)?
fields.back: Up to roughly 96%.
fields.extra: Rates varied widely across models and scenario variants; the headline number is the ceiling, not the average.

fields.front: How did behavior change in Agentic Misalignment when models stated they suspected a test?
fields.back: They misbehaved less than when they treated the scenario as real.
fields.extra: Evaluation awareness confounds safety evals: measured behavior may understate deployed behavior.

fields.front: What did AI Organizations (Shen et al. 2026) find about multi-agent systems?
fields.back: Organizations of AI agents are more effective than individual agents but less aligned.
fields.extra: Composition is not alignment-preserving; oversight designed for single agents does not automatically transfer.

fields.front: What is TruthfulQA?
fields.back: A benchmark of questions where humans commonly hold false beliefs, testing whether models imitating human text reproduce those falsehoods.
fields.extra: Owain Evans' most-cited benchmark.

fields.front: What was TruthfulQA's headline finding about model scale at release?
fields.back: Larger models were often *less* truthful: imitative falsehood scales with capability.
fields.extra: The canonical example that imitation and truthfulness diverge.

fields.front: What is the Reversal Curse?
fields.back: Models trained on 'A is B' fail to infer 'B is A': a model can know Tom Cruise's mother is Mary Lee Pfeiffer yet fail to answer who Mary Lee Pfeiffer's son is.
fields.extra: Facts are stored directionally, in the order training text presents them; a basic asymmetry of gradient-trained knowledge.

fields.front: What is out-of-context reasoning?
fields.back: Using facts learned during training at inference time without those facts appearing in the prompt and without in-context examples.
fields.extra: E.g., a model finetuned on third-person descriptions of a chatbot later acts on that knowledge unprompted. From 'Taken out of context' (Berglund et al.): the building block of situational awareness and of data-poisoning-style threat models.

fields.front: What did 'Connecting the Dots' demonstrate?
fields.back: LLMs can aggregate evidence scattered across many training documents to infer and verbalize the latent structure that generated them - inductive inference beyond memorization.
fields.extra: E.g., recovering an underlying function or identity. Why filtering explicit statements of a dangerous fact from training data may not prevent the model from knowing it.

fields.front: What is the two-hop latent reasoning problem?
fields.back: Models that separately know A->B and B->C often fail to compose them into A->C without writing out the intermediate step.
fields.extra: Apparent successes frequently trace to co-occurrence in training data rather than genuine latent composition. From Lessons from Studying Two-Hop Latent Reasoning (Balesni et al.); a capability boundary that CoT papers over.

fields.front: What does the Situational Awareness Dataset (SAD) measure?
fields.back: Whether models know they are models and can act on that self-knowledge.
fields.extra: Subskills: recognizing their own generated text, knowing their interfaces and constraints. Situational awareness is dual-use: needed for reliable agents, prerequisite for evaluation gaming and scheming.

fields.front: How does the black-box lie detector in 'How to Catch an AI Liar' work?
fields.back: After a suspected lie, ask a fixed battery of unrelated yes/no follow-up questions; a simple classifier on the answer pattern detects lying and generalizes across models and contexts.
fields.extra: Lying leaves a downstream behavioral signature even when you never see weights or activations.

fields.front: What did 'Base Models Know How to Reason, Thinking Models Learn When' find?
fields.back: The reasoning behaviors of thinking models already exist in their base models; much of post-training's effect is teaching *when* to deploy them.
fields.extra: Activating base-model mechanisms at the right moments recovers a large share of the thinking model's performance gap. Reframes reasoning post-training as elicitation and scheduling rather than capability creation.

fields.front: What is the headline of Inverse Scaling in Test-Time Compute (Gema et al. 2025)?
fields.back: More thinking is not monotonically better: extended reasoning can amplify flawed heuristics and worsen performance.
fields.extra: Failure modes found: Claude models get distracted by irrelevant information; OpenAI o-series overfit to familiar problem framings; regression tasks shift to spurious correlations; deduction degrades on constraint tracking; some safety behaviors worsen.

fields.front: In Inverse Scaling in Test-Time Compute, what failure did Claude models show as reasoning grew longer?
fields.back: They became increasingly distracted by irrelevant information in the prompt.
fields.extra: Contrast hook: the o-series failure was overfitting to familiar problem framings, not distraction.

fields.front: What safety-relevant behavior change did Inverse Scaling in Test-Time Compute observe with longer reasoning?
fields.back: Some safety behaviors worsened, e.g. Claude Sonnet 4 expressed more self-preservation.
fields.extra: Extended reasoning is not automatically safer reasoning.

fields.front: What phenomenon does Chunky Post-Training (Murray et al. 2026) document?
fields.back: Generalization failures caused by post-training data arriving in correlated chunks rather than well-mixed.
fields.extra: Practical reminder that data ordering and mixing are alignment-relevant hyperparameters, not plumbing.

fields.front: What alternative to pretraining data filtering does Shilov et al. 2025 propose for dangerous capabilities?
fields.back: Knowledge localization: find where a capability lives in the weights and remove it there, instead of (or beyond) filtering the training data.
fields.extra: Companion result: token-level data filtering (Rathi and Radford 2026) shapes capabilities at finer granularity than document filtering.

fields.front: Before evaluating a clever method, what must you run?
fields.back: The dumbest plausible baseline on the same data and metric.
fields.extra: Most apparent wins disappear against a tuned simple baseline. In interp: compare your fancy vector against a random direction and a difference-of-means before claiming structure.

fields.front: What does a positive control prove in an evaluation?
fields.back: That the instrument can detect the effect: a condition where the effect must appear.
fields.extra: Without it, a null result might just be instrument failure. Hook: positive control protects your *negatives*.

fields.front: What does a negative control bound in an evaluation?
fields.back: False positives: a condition where the effect must not appear.
fields.extra: Without it, a positive result might just be noise. Hook: negative control protects your *positives*.

fields.front: When de-risking a new research idea, which experiment do you run first?
fields.back: The cheapest one that could kill the idea.
fields.extra: Maximize information per unit time, not thoroughness per experiment. Most fellows papers are one crisp kill-test that survived, then got scaled and controlled.

fields.front: What is the highest-value, lowest-status activity in empirical LLM research?
fields.back: Reading raw transcripts and samples.
fields.extra: Most bugs, confounds, and judge failures are visible in ten examples before any statistics. Schedule it: read N random transcripts per experiment, including from the conditions that worked.

fields.front: Before trusting an LLM judge in your eval, what do you do?
fields.back: Hand-label a random sample and measure agreement, checking false-positive and false-negative rates separately.
fields.extra: Report judge accuracy alongside judged results. Judges inherit the biases of their model family; a judge from the same family as the policy is a confound.

fields.front: Your intervention improved the metric by 2%. What is the first question to ask?
fields.back: What is the run-to-run variance across seeds and sampling?
fields.extra: No claim until the effect exceeds the noise floor measured on identical conditions. For finetuning experiments, seed variance is often larger than published effect sizes.

fields.front: What is the honest way to present qualitative transcript examples?
fields.back: State the denominator and selection rule: how many were generated, whether shown examples are random or curated, and include failure cases.
fields.extra: A cherry-picked transcript with no denominator is an anecdote, not evidence.

fields.front: What does a well-scoped 4-month fellowship project look like?
fields.back: One crisp question, one model organism or benchmark, one clean intervention with controls, and one figure that could stand alone.
fields.extra: Cut everything that does not feed the main claim. Reverse-engineered from the fellows papers themselves: nearly all of them fit this shape.
